docs.text: Feature Description Syntax diagnostics Compilation errors reported as you type Hover Documentation for keywords, built-in types, and user-defined symbols Go to definition Jump to the definition of a user-defined symbol Code completion Keywords, built-ins, modules, and user-defined symbols Document symbols Outline view of classes, interfaces, methods, enums, records, and fields Workspace symbols Search symbols across all open documents Signature help Method signatures while typing a call Rename symbol Rename occurrences of the symbol at the cursor in the current file

docs.title: Symbol support
docs.text:
The language server indexes the following symbols from open documents:

  • Classes, interfaces, enums, and records
  • Methods (def)
  • Class-level fields (val / var)
  • Method arguments and local variables

Symbol information is used for completion, hover, go-to-definition, and both document and workspace symbol requests.

docs.title: VSCode integration
docs.text: Install the vscode-onion extension. It will automatically start onion-lsp when you open an .on file and connect it to the editor.

docs.title: REPL
docs.text: The Onion REPL is implemented by onion.tools.Repl and is the primary interactive shell for the language.

docs.title: Starting the REPL
docs.text:
Use any of the following entry points:

# Development
sbt repl

# From a distribution or local bin/
onion repl
onion-repl

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Multi-line input with continuation prompts
  • Session-scoped module, import, val, var, def, and class declarations
  • Persistent resN bindings for evaluated expressions
  • History and syntax highlighting through JLine 3
  • Session compile caching for repeated inspections of the same source snapshot

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
:help                 Show help
:quit / :exit / :q    Exit the REPL
:clear                Clear the terminal
:history              Show input history
:imports              Show current module/imports
:type <expr>          Show the inferred type of an expression
:ast <expr>           Dump the parsed AST for an expression
:typed <expr>         Dump the typed AST summary for an expression
:bytecode <expr>      Dump generated JVM bytecode for an expression
:load <file>          Load a file into the current session
:time [on|off]        Toggle compile/eval timing
:classpath            Show the active classpath
:reset                Reset the session
:paste                Enter paste mode

docs.title: Results and resN
docs.text:
onion> 2 + 2
res0: Int = 4

onion> res0 * 10
res1: Int = 40

docs.title: Multi-line Definitions
docs.text:
onion> def factorial(n: Int): Int {
     |   if n <= 1 {
     |     return 1;
     |   } else {
     |     return n * factorial(n - 1);
     |   }
     | }

docs.title: Inspecting Types and Bytecode
docs.text:
onion> :type Math::max(1, 2)
Math::max(1, 2): Int

onion> :bytecode 1 + 2
== <generated class> ==
...

docs.title: Loading Files
docs.text:
onion> :load run/Hello.on
Hello

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• onion repl and onion-repl start the same implementation.
  • :time is useful when comparing REPL compile/eval latency while working on compiler performance.
  • :bytecode renders class output with ASM tracing, so helper/closure classes may appear alongside the primary generated class.

docs.title: Script Runner (onion)
docs.text: The onion command compiles and executes Onion source files directly in memory, without creating .class files.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: onion [options] source files... [program arguments]

docs.title: -classpath <classpath>
docs.text:
Set the classpath for compilation and execution.

onion -classpath lib/mylib.jar MyScript.on

docs.title: -encoding <encoding>
docs.text:
Specify the character encoding of source files.

onion -encoding UTF-8 MyScript.on

docs.title: -maxErrorReports <count>
docs.text:
Limit the number of compilation errors reported.

onion -maxErrorReports 10 MyScript.on

docs.title: --dump-ast
docs.text:
Print the parsed AST to stderr before running the script.

onion --dump-ast MyScript.on

docs.title: --dump-typed-ast
docs.text:
Print a typed AST summary (classes, fields, methods) to stderr before running the script.

onion --dump-typed-ast MyScript.on

docs.title: --profile-compile
docs.text:
Emit a compile profile before the script is executed.

onion --profile-compile MyScript.on

docs.title: --profile-format <text|json>
docs.text:
Choose text or JSON profile output.

onion --profile-compile --profile-format json MyScript.on

docs.title: --profile-output <target>
docs.text:
Write the compile profile to stderr, stdout, or a file path.

onion --profile-compile --profile-format json \
      --profile-output target/script-profile.json \
      MyScript.on

docs.title: --warn <off|on|error>
docs.text:
Control warning reporting. error treats warnings as compilation errors.

onion --warn error MyScript.on

docs.title: --Wno <codes>
docs.text:
Suppress specific warning categories by code or name.

onion --Wno W0001,unused-parameter MyScript.on

docs.title: Program Arguments
docs.text:
Arguments after the source file(s) are passed to your program:

onion MyScript.on arg1 arg2 arg3

Access them in your code:

class MyScript {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      foreach arg :String in args {
        println("Argument: " + arg)
      }
    }
}

docs.title: Entry Point
docs.text: The script runner determines the entry point automatically:

docs.title: 1. Explicit Main Method
docs.text:
If a class has a main method, it's used as the entry point:

class MyProgram {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      println("Hello from main method")
    }
}

docs.title: 2. First Class with Main
docs.text:
If multiple classes have main methods, the first one is used:

class First {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      println("This will run")
    }
}

class Second {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      println("This won't run")
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Top-Level Declarations and Expressions
docs.text:
If there's no explicit main method, the first top-level declaration or expression is the entry point:

println("Hello, World!")

val x: Int = 10
println("x = " + x)

// These block elements execute immediately

docs.title: Simple Script
docs.text:
hello.on: 

println("Hello, World!")

Run: 

$ onion hello.on
Hello, World!

docs.title: With Arguments
docs.text:
greet.on: 

class Greeter {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      if args.length > 0 {
        println("Hello, " + args[0] + "!")
      } else {
        println("Hello, stranger!")
      }
    }
}

Run: 

$ onion greet.on Alice
Hello, Alice!

$ onion greet.on
Hello, stranger!

docs.title: Quick Calculations
docs.text:
calc.on: 

val a: Int = 10
val b: Int = 20
println("Sum: " + (a + b))
println("Product: " + (a * b))

Run: 

$ onion calc.on
Sum: 30
Product: 200

docs.title: File Processing
docs.text:
count_lines.on: 

import {
  java.io.BufferedReader;
  java.io.FileReader;
}

class LineCounter {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      if args.length == 0 {
        println("Usage: onion count_lines.on <filename>")
        return
      }

      val filename: String = args[0]
      val reader: BufferedReader = new BufferedReader(
        new FileReader(filename)
      )

      var count: Int = 0
      var line: String = null
      while (line = reader.readLine()) != null {
        count = count + 1
      }

      reader.close()
      println("Lines: " + count)
    }
}

Run: 

$ onion count_lines.on data.txt
Lines: 42

docs.title: In-Memory Compilation
docs.text:
The onion command:

  1. Compiles source files to bytecode
  2. Loads classes into memory
  3. Executes the entry point
  4. No .class files are created

This is ideal for: - Quick scripts - Testing code snippets - Automation tasks - One-off programs

docs.title: Multiple Source Files
docs.text:
Compile and run multiple files:

onion Main.on Utils.on Helper.on

All files are compiled together, and the entry point is determined from the first file.

docs.title: Compilation Errors
docs.text:
$ onion bad_syntax.on
Error: Type mismatch at bad_syntax.on:5
Compilation failed

docs.title: Runtime Errors
docs.text:
$ onion runtime_error.on
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
    at RuntimeError.main(runtime_error.on:10)

docs.title: Comparison with onionc
docs.text: Feature onion onionc Creates .class files No Yes Execution Immediate Requires java command Use case Scripts, testing Production, libraries Speed Fast for small programs Better for repeated runs Distribution Requires source Can distribute .class/.jar

docs.title: Shebang Line (Unix-like systems)
docs.text:
Make scripts executable:

hello.on: 

#!/usr/bin/env onion
println("Hello from script!")

Make executable: 

chmod +x hello.on
./hello.on

docs.title: Error Messages
docs.text:
Provide helpful error messages:

class Script {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      if args.length < 2 {
        println("Error: Missing arguments")
        println("Usage: onion script.on <input> <output>")
        return
      }

      // Process arguments...
    }
}

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
Return appropriate exit codes:

class Script {
  public:
    static def main(args :String[]): void {
      if args.length == 0 {
        System::exit(1)  // Error
      }

      // Success
      System::exit(0)
    }
}

docs.text:
• Compiler (onionc) - Compile to class files
  • REPL Shell - Interactive programming
  • Examples - Example scripts
